proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Strong Steam store text describes a hybrid centered on building and governing a colony/state apparatus, expanding facilities, researching technologies, automating production and logistics, managing population conditions, and then personally leading first-person roguelite combat expeditions. That supports colony_sim as the best-fit primary identity, with military_fps and RTS-like command elements as secondary influences. Evidence is somewhat noisy because the Metacritic description appears mismatched to another game, but the Steam detailed and short descriptions are concrete and substantial enough on their own to support acceptance.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Pairs base expansion, tech progression, and resource logistics with active real-time combat in a hostile sci-fi world.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Rotwood as a 1-4 player hack-and-slash roguelike/dungeon crawler built around repeat runs, co-op combat, boss fights, weapon mastery, gear upgrading, and bringing spoils back to craft equipment and improve a safe haven. The text explicitly says 'every run offers different powers and abilities,' calls it a 'rogue-like dungeon crawler,' emphasizes combat mastery over randomness, and highlights increasingly difficult encounters and powerful corrupted bosses. Metacritic also labels it a '2D Beat-'Em-Up,' which supports the side-scrolling/beat-em-up secondary read, but the stronger identity is cooperative action roguelite due to run-based structure, repeatable progression, and meta/base advancement.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: 1-4 player co-op action roguelite with hack-and-slash combat, weapon/build experimentation, room-to-room runs, and boss-driven progression.
Strong match on co-op run-based action, distinct combat styles, replayable builds, and difficult fights, though it is more top-down roguelike and less brawler-like than Rotwood.
Excellent comp for the 4-player side-scrolling hack-and-slash/brawler identity, with boss-heavy stage progression; less roguelite and build/run-focused than Rotwood.
Shares co-op-capable action RPG roguelite structure, persistent progression, and dungeon runs, but it is more story-driven and character-switching ARPG than combat-mastery brawler.
4-player co-op side-scrolling action roguelite with brawler-style combat, run-based progression, loot/build variation, and escalating boss fights.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DLC descriptions consistently describe a combat-centric action game built around three new campaign chapters, unique levels, new weapons, bosses, fiend enemies, deadly trials, and an endurance-style challenge mode with successive combat encounters. The weapon copy emphasizes ranged attack, crowd control, rapid strikes, closing distance, and testing skilled 'master ninja' play, which strongly supports a fast, mastery-heavy character-action identity. The campaign/chapter structure and boss/trial language support linear level-based progression rather than open-world or RPG-first play. Horror imagery is present in the setting and story framing, but the gameplay evidence is much more strongly centered on stylish high-intensity combat than on horror as the primary play identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: This DLC directly inherits the base game's core mission-based campaign structure, weapon-driven combo combat, enemy gauntlets, and challenge-focused progression.
A premier character-action comp with stylish combo chaining, distinct weapon kits, tough encounters, and score-attack style replayability.
Shares the same fast, skill-intensive character-action loop centered on dodging, combo expression, set-piece fights, and combat challenge content.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player hand-drawn narrative adventure with puzzles, rhythm segments, minigames, light action, and character-focused mystery. However, the copy does not establish a stable core gameplay loop, perspective, traversal model, or dominant mechanics strongly enough to support any allowed V2 archetype. It is not clearly a visual novel, hidden-object puzzle game, action-platformer, psychological horror title, or cinematic action-adventure based on the available text. The gameplay appears intentionally mixed and provider copy stays broad, so the evidence is insufficient for a confident archetype assignment.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very good match on whimsical character-driven adventure, exploration, puzzle solving, and music-led interactions with emotional payoff; its platforming emphasis is a mild mismatch.
Strong fit for narrative-first exploration, rich NPC interactions, minigame variety, and slow-burn darker themes, though it is lighter on puzzle/rhythm-centric progression than Apopia.

Shares quirky writing, emotional mystery, and conflict resolved through distinctive encounter mechanics, but its core identity is much more RPG/bullet-dodge combat driven than Apopia's broader adventure-minigame structure.

Good tonal and narrative match with cute-creepy mystery and choice-driven story discovery, but its branching storybook structure is closer to interactive narrative than Apopia's puzzle/rhythm/light-action mix.
Story-rich adventure with dialogue, puzzles, light action, and surreal mind-based worlds that turn inner thoughts into playable spaces.

title: Scott Pilgrim EX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Classification is strongly supported by the stored store/editorial copy plus a direct Metacritic genre label of '2D Beat-'Em-Up.' The text repeatedly frames the game as a 'brand-new brawling adventure' centered on fighting through Toronto, stringing together stylish combos, using weapons and special attacks, battling enemies and bosses, and supporting up to four-player co-op. It also specifies a 2D beat-'em-up identity, seven fighters with distinct combat styles, and a city of interconnected levels with quests and hidden areas, which fits a campaign-driven beat_em_up with semi-open progression and light action-adventure structure rather than a pure linear stage arcade brawler. Progression is reinforced by badges, stat upgrades, coins, and customizable builds.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Co-op side-scrolling brawler with quests, shops, character progression, and an explorable connected city structure rather than just isolated stages.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: Shredder's Revenge
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: Shredder's Revenge
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Modern arcade-style co-op beat-'em-up focused on combos, multiple fighters, crowd control, and approachable drop-in multiplayer.
Another high-profile modern beat-'em-up centered on combo routing, distinct characters, special attacks, and local/online co-op play.
Four-player side-scrolling brawler with light RPG progression, build customization, and chaotic co-op combat that overlaps with Scott Pilgrim EX's party-play loop.

unresolved_similar_games: Scott Pilgrim vs. The World: The Game – Complete Edition
missing_must_include_titles: Scott Pilgrim vs. The World: The Game – Complete Edition

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Esoteric Ebb as an isometric single-player CRPG with deep branching dialogue, abundant choices, open-ended quests, dice-driven TTRPG-style encounters, investigation of a political conspiracy, and exploration of a fantasy city. The strongest identity signals are authored branching narrative, quest-solving, detective-mystery framing, and classic CRPG structure rather than party tactics or action combat. Combat is explicitly de-emphasized ('violence as a last resort'), so western_narrative_rpg fits better than party_based_crpg or combat-forward RPG archetypes.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest comp: isometric detective CRPG with skill voices in your head, heavy dialogue checks, political mystery, and failure-forward roleplaying.
Classic dialogue-driven CRPG focused on bizarre worldbuilding, philosophical quests, and solving problems through conversation more than combat.
Modern Torment-style RPG built around text-heavy encounters, stat checks, consequential choices, and exploration of a strange setting.
Also emphasizes dice-driven checks, investigation, faction pressures, and branching narrative progression over traditional combat-heavy RPG structure.
Strong fit for its authored mystery-solving, dialogue-led roleplay, and consequential social investigation, even though it is less of a full CRPG.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store copy is strong and concrete: the game is repeatedly described as a 'third-person arcade racer' focused on licensed car racing, online races, global time trials, and weekly events. Core play is competing in races, chasing lap times, and progressing through a motorsport legacy structure by earning resources and investing in team growth, but the identity remains vehicular racing first. The 'easy to learn yet endlessly rewarding' physics, stylized recreations of licensed cars/tracks, and explicit arcade-racer phrasing support arcade_racer over realistic_racer, while the management layer appears secondary rather than transforming it into a sim/tycoon game.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best fit: accessible arcade-style motorsport racing with career seasons, upgrades, staff/team management, time trials, and on-track/off-track decision making very close to the anchor's loop.

Strong circuit-racing and multiplayer overlap, with accessible handling and career structure, but it is more spectacle-driven simcade racing and lacks the anchor's team-building/resource-management emphasis.
Stylized circuit racer with approachable handling, motorsport discipline variety, career-style event structure, and strong time-trial focus.
Arcade motorsport progression built around earning money, buying cars, and advancing through event ladders rather than pure sim racing.
Explicitly arcade handling on licensed-style stock car racing with event progression and a brighter, less sim-heavy presentation.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently depict a third-person single-player action-adventure campaign centered on two protagonists with distinct combat abilities, battling enemies through ruined gothic spaces while solving environmental puzzles and progressing through an authored dark-fantasy story. The text strongly supports melee/supernatural combat, handcrafted linear-to-semi-open level spaces, puzzle gating, and long-form campaign structure, which best fits cinematic_action_adventure. A light action_platformer adjacency is present due to traversal/platform navigation, but platforming does not appear primary.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Legacy of Kain: Soul Reaver 1 & 2 Remastered
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close in feel: story-driven 3D action-adventure, spectral-themed powers, environmental puzzles, and linear exploration centered on Raziel.
A recognizable third-person action-adventure comp with melee combat, dungeon-like progression, environmental puzzles, and gothic-fantasy exploration.
Another linear dark-fantasy action-adventure blending weapon combat, platforming, and environmental puzzle sequences across large atmospheric spaces.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 'classic single-screen platformer'/'2D platformer' with '100 lovingly designed screens,' explicit 'jump and run' play, local 2-player couch co-op, and arcade-inspired leaderboard chasing. The copy also states players 'finish off the numerous enemies with their very own challenges and tactics' and confront the villain Grabbo, which is enough to establish meaningful combat alongside dominant platform traversal. This supports action_platformer over other platformer families: it is clearly not a metroidvania, not a 3D collectathon, and the emphasis is broader arcade stage-clearing rather than precision-only challenge branding.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: single-screen/compact stage arcade platforming, enemy-focused clears, level-based structure, and strong couch co-op overlap. One of the clearest comps for Chickums.
Tight single-screen platform combat with excellent couch multiplayer, though it is more versus/arena-focused than stage-clearing arcade platforming.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently describe a single-player, third-person cozy gardening game set in historical France. The core loop is restoring and tending a garden in a free-form natural space, growing and identifying plants through simulated ecosystem factors like soil, moisture, and plant proximity, while progressing gentle quests, uncovering hidden objects, helping NPCs, and pursuing romance. The game is explicitly peaceful and non-combat, with linear story advancement layered onto an at-your-own-pace life/gardening routine. That aligns best with farming_sim, with life_sim as a secondary influence due to social/romance and community-help elements.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest match on free-form gardening, restoring an overgrown space, relaxed pacing, neighbor tasks, unlocking tools/seeds/areas, and cozy non-combat progression.
Strong cozy life-sim overlap via gardening, helping townsfolk, romance, and story progression, though it is a fuller farming/crafting sim with more systems than the anchor.

Good fit on calm plant-growing, historical setting, exploration, and figuring out ideal growth conditions, but it is primarily a first-person environmental puzzle game rather than an ongoing gardening/life sim.

Reasonable broad cozy-sim comp through crop growing, restoration, quests, and relationships, but its open-ended farm management, combat/mine loop, and multiplayer profile make it less like the anchor’s focused peaceful garden restoration.
Third-person cozy restoration game focused on nurturing natural spaces, unlocking new areas, and helping a community rather than running a hard-economy farm.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored text focuses almost entirely on setting, zones, and story context for Azeroth/Quel'Thalas/Xal'atath, without concrete gameplay loop, combat, progression, perspective, or mode details. The only direct gameplay-identity signal is a single Metacritic genre label of 'MMORPG', which is not sufficient on its own to confidently map this specific release to an allowed archetype such as mmo_action_rpg, especially since the record is not marked as DLC and therefore cannot inherit World of Warcraft parent evidence under the provided rules.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: World of Warcraft: The War Within
staged_neighbors.requested_title: World of Warcraft: The War Within
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another contemporary WoW expansion with the same core MMO structure, class kit expectations, shared-world progression, and expansion/endgame audience.
Very strong theme-park MMORPG comp: quest-led expansion content, role-based group PvE, long-term character progression, and live-service endgame.
Still fundamentally World of Warcraft MMO gameplay, but Classic's older combat pacing, progression friction, and endgame model make it a poorer stand-in than modern retail WoW content.
Clear fantasy MMORPG neighbor with persistent progression and group PvE, though its action-heavier combat and more solo-friendly structure are looser matches.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Star Wars: The Old Republic – Onslaught
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Theme-park MMORPG with hotbar combat, quest hubs, instanced group content, classes, and long-form online character progression.

title: Lil Gator Game: Gator of the Year

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence is insufficient for classification. The bundle contains no gameplay description, no taxonomy text corpus, no genre/theme/mode labels with usable semantic meaning, and no parent-game context to inherit from. Available data is limited to platform labels and opaque theme IDs, which do not establish gameplay identity strongly enough for Taxonomy V2.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest base-game match if this edition builds directly on the original’s exploration, climbing, gliding, questing, and toy-combat collectathon loop.
Compact exploration adventure focused on traversal, chatting with characters, light objectives, and a relaxed discovery-first structure.
Cute low-stakes 3D platforming and exploration with light quests, collectibles, and gentle movement-based progression.
Shares the cozy roaming, helping-people quest loop, and upbeat exploration-driven progression, though with less platforming emphasis.
Small-scale wholesome exploration game built around meeting characters, completing tasks, and steadily unlocking more of the play space.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Soulshard as a 2D side-scrolling platformer built around 30 discrete levels, trap avoidance, and using the environment to progress, with single-player and local co-op play. The copy does not establish meaningful combat, RPG systems, exploration structure, or action-platformer fighting, which points away from action_platformer and toward a non-combat precision/platform-navigation identity. Minimalist pixel-art presentation and a dark limbo-like setting are also directly supported by the store text.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on 2D co-op platforming, coordinated traversal, hazard navigation, and level-based structure; more puzzle-specialized and more explicitly co-op-dependent than Soulshard.
Strong fit on side-scrolling traversal, environmental obstacles, local co-op plus solo play, and low-combat challenge structure; less minimalist and more cinematic than the anchor.
Good match for trap-heavy stage challenges, precise movement, solo/couch co-op support, and platform-focused difficulty; somewhat more twitchy and puzzle-stunt driven.

Fits teamwork platforming, obstacle-course progression, and environmental interaction well, but is more gimmick-led and more strongly built around two-character cooperation than Soulshard seems to be.
2D puzzle-platformer with solo or local co-op, light/no combat focus, and progression built around hazard navigation and environmental cooperation.

title: Pokémon FireRed Version and Pokémon LeafGreen Version

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a classic Pokémon RPG centered on capturing wild Pokémon, building and training a collection, and battling Gym Leaders across the Kanto region. The text explicitly mentions turn-based gameplay, collecting the original 151 Pokémon, earning badges, developing battle strategies, and progressing through an authored regional adventure. That combination strongly matches monster_collect_rpg, with jrpg_story_rpg as a secondary fit due to its linear campaign structure and heroic journey framing.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Pokemon Brilliant Diamond and Shining Pearl
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Pokémon Brilliant Diamond and Pokémon Shining Pearl
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another faithful remake pair built around classic mainline Pokémon structure: route-based exploration, turn-based battles, Gym badges, and Pokédex completion.

unresolved_similar_games: Pokémon HeartGold Version and Pokémon SoulSilver Version
Pokémon Ruby Version and Pokémon Sapphire Version
Pokémon Red Version and Pokémon Blue Version
Pokémon: Let's Go, Pikachu! and Let's Go, Eevee!
missing_must_include_titles: Pokémon HeartGold Version and Pokémon SoulSilver Version
Pokémon Ruby Version and Pokémon Sapphire Version
Pokémon Red Version and Pokémon Blue Version

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies DRACU-RIOT! as a single-player visual novel/adventure with no combat emphasis and a linear authored story. Metacritic explicitly labels it 'Visual Novel'; Steam and the corpus provide only story/setup text centered on protagonist, characters, and setting, with no signs of action, strategy, simulation, or puzzle-forward play. The gameplay identity is therefore best matched as visual_novel.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another Yuzusoft romance VN built around heroine routes, character-driven comedy, and a secret supernatural/sci-fi premise rather than gameplay systems.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Café Stella and the Reaper's Butterflies
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Café Stella and the Reaper's Butterflies
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Route-based romance visual novel with supernatural elements, slice-of-life scenes, and a similar balance of light comedy, mystery, and heroine-focused progression.
A highly recognizable modern romance VN with branching heroine routes, strong character focus, and mostly linear reading-based play with no combat.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Aokana - Four Rhythms Across the Blue
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Aokana - Four Rhythms Across the Blue
staged_neighbors.why_similar: A popular choice-driven romance VN with multiple heroine paths and heavy emphasis on reading, character interactions, and authored story structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently portray Aquamarine as a single-player, turn-based sci-fi survival/exploration adventure focused on foraging, cultivation, salvage, loadout upgrades, navigational challenges, and environmental puzzle solving in an alien ocean. The strongest supported signals are cursor-driven/point-and-click interaction, authored linear mystery framing, non-combat play, and environmental puzzles. However, none of the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes cleanly fits this combination. It is not well-supported as hidden_object_puzzle because the evidence emphasizes survival, navigation, and environmental problem solving rather than hidden-object scenes. It is not well-supported as management_tycoon because there is no strong business/optimization/tycoon loop. It also does not cleanly map to exploration_survival_adventure because the available archetype implies a more standard real-time survival-adventure identity than the game's turn-based, comic-book, puzzle-forward structure. Because the evidence is solid about what the game is, but does not strongly support any allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best fit here: turn-based expedition structure, survival pressure, resource management, procedural hazards, and roguelike run tension align well, though it is more party/event driven and less puzzle-centric than Aquamarine.

Shares journey-based survival, supply management, hazard mitigation, and authored-progress campaign framing. It is less about environmental puzzle solving and alien-world exploration, but the survival-travel loop is meaningfully adjacent.

Very strong overlap in alien-ocean survival, scavenging, crafting upgrades, studying wildlife, and trying to get home. It falls short on gameplay identity because Subnautica is real-time, open-world, and more action-driven than Aquamarine's turn-based, puzzle-forward structure.
Exploration-led sci-fi mystery with low combat, deliberate navigation, and puzzle-like discovery, though it is more narrative-linguistic than survival-focused.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions repeatedly frame the game as 'survival horror' and add concrete loop details: combat, investigations, puzzles, and resource management. The corpus also explicitly says players can freely switch between first- and third-person views, supporting a modern single-player campaign survival-horror structure rather than a purely cinematic or purely psychological horror title. Story setup emphasizes investigating mysterious deaths in Raccoon City while escaping lethal threats, which supports detective_mystery plus survival_escape within an authored linear campaign.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: A very close match on core loop: tense exploration, limited resources, puzzle solving, authored campaigns, and survival-horror combat pressure in a modern Resident Evil format.

Strong modern RE survival-horror comp with inventory pressure, linear campaign progression, and combat/horror balance, though it leans more action-forward than the anchor’s classic investigative framing.
Good third-person survival-horror neighbor with combat, stealth, limited ammo, exploration, and narrative progression, but its semi-open structure and stealth/trap emphasis make it a looser fit.
Relevant especially for Requiem’s Leon/action-horror side, but RE4’s set-piece pacing and heavier combat emphasis sit somewhat farther from classic puzzle-gated survival-horror identity.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Core Resident Evil survival-horror loop: scarce resources, puzzle-gated exploration, tense combat, and authored escape-focused progression.

removed_candidates: Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Laysara: Summit Kingdom as a 'challenging city builder' and a 'pure city building experience' focused on economy, resource management, production chains, settlement expansion, transport/logistics optimization, hazard mitigation, and rebuilding across multiple mountain towns. The core loop is establishing and expanding settlements, satisfying population needs, building trade and transport networks, and solving environmental constraints like avalanches and weather. The text also directly states there is no combat or military aspect. This strongly supports city_builder as the primary archetype, with management_tycoon/colony_sim as adjacent secondary readings due to resource chains, needs management, and multi-settlement administration.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on city-building fundamentals: multiple settlements, hostile weather, needs balancing, production chains, and persistent network-level progression. The main mismatch is its roguelite run structure versus Laysara's steadier sandbox/campaign buildout.

Excellent fit on economy design, citizen-need ladders, inter-settlement trade, and logistics optimization. It is less survival-focused and includes diplomacy/warfare and broader empire play, but the production-network identity is very relevant.

Strong overlap in terrain-aware building, infrastructure routing, environmental hazard preparation, and sandbox settlement optimization. It is more colony-survival and water/terraforming-driven than Laysara's multi-town mountain trade focus.

A solid combat-free settlement survival builder with resource planning, population management, and town sustainability. It is a simpler, more traditional single-settlement survival sim with less emphasis on transport engineering and interconnected settlements.
Deep logistics-first city building with intricate transport networks, production chains, and infrastructure optimization over combat.

title: The New Zealand Story: Untold Adventure

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a colorful 2D platformer and remake of the 1988 platformer The NewZealand Story. The gameplay loop is described as jumping and running through handcrafted levels, uncovering hidden areas, using weapons, vehicles, and power-ups, and facing challenging enemies and bosses. This supports a level-based side-scrolling action-platformer identity with platform navigation as the core verb and combat as a supporting but meaningful layer. Although the computed near-miss/candidate system surfaced beat_em_up from side-scrolling + melee + bosses, the text strongly centers platforming, responsive movement, precision, hidden secrets, and arcade stage challenge rather than crowd-control brawling, making action_platformer the better fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: arcade-origin 2D action-platforming, stage clears, projectile combat, hazards, and boss encounters align strongly with the anchor’s core loop.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Alex Kidd in Miracle World DX
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Alex Kidd in Miracle World DX
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another classic-platformer revival with level-based progression, boss fights, old-school difficulty, and modernized controls; slightly looser due to its distinctive rock-paper-scissors boss structure.
Excellent 2D action-platformer fundamentals with responsive movement, secrets, and level-based play, but it is more fluid and modern set-piece driven than the anchor’s retro arcade challenge profile.
Modern revival of a classic 2D action-platformer with precision movement, enemies, secrets, and boss-gated progression.
Stage-based side-scrolling platforming focused on hazards, enemy encounters, exploration, and memorable boss fights.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is strong and direct for a visual novel classification. Multiple store/editorial descriptions explicitly call it a 'visual novel' and describe '60k of text,' '2-4 hours of reading,' and 'a linear narrative with no choices or branches.' The gameplay identity is therefore narrative-reading focused rather than action, strategy, or exploration driven. Supporting facets include single-player mode, cute anime-style presentation, casual genre labeling, and absence of combat mechanics. The premise centers on character interaction between the demon queen and princess, supporting an authored linear interpersonal drama structure within a fantasy setting.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: A well-known yuri visual novel built around reading, relationship development, and lighthearted character interactions rather than branching mechanics or action.
A recognizable romance-first yuri VN with a relaxed, story-led structure and a strong focus on dialogue, chemistry, and emotional payoff.
Another anime-style yuri visual novel where the core loop is reading character scenes and following relationship drama in a mostly traditional VN format.
